frontier_justification: AI-driven retrosynthesis prediction is one of the most impactful applications of deep learning in chemistry (2017-2025). The field has evolved from LSTM sequence models through template-based neural networks and graph-native methods to billion-scale generative pretrained transformers, with RSGPT (2025, Nature Communications) achieving 63.4% top-1 accuracy through pretraining on 10 billion synthetic reaction datapoints — representing a paradigm shift toward LLM-scale chemistry models.

papers.title: Retrosynthetic Reaction Prediction Using Neural Sequence-to-Sequence Models
papers.key_contribution: First neural sequence-to-sequence model for retrosynthesis: treats retrosynthetic prediction as a machine translation problem from product SMILES to reactant SMILES using LSTM encoder-decoder; achieves 37.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k, comparable to rule-based expert systems.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Retrosynthesis planning — predicting which reactants produce a target molecule — traditionally relies on hand-coded reaction rules (expert systems) that are brittle, incomplete, and require extensive human curation of chemical knowledge.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: LSTM-based encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence model; product molecules represented as SMILES strings (input) are translated to reactant SMILES (output); trained on 50,000 reactions from USPTO patent literature spanning 10 reaction types.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 37.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k benchmark, performing comparably to rule-based expert systems (35.4%); demonstrates that retrosynthesis can be framed as a data-driven sequence translation problem without explicit chemical rules.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: LSTM encoder-decoder lacks attention mechanism and struggles with long SMILES strings; limited to single-step retrosynthesis; no mechanism for handling stereochemistry; accuracy insufficient for practical synthesis planning.

papers.title: Computer-Assisted Retrosynthesis Based on Molecular Similarity
papers.key_contribution: Template-based neural retrosynthesis using molecular fingerprint similarity: instead of generating SMILES strings, the model ranks known reaction templates by comparing molecular fingerprints of the query product to precedent reactions; achieves 74.1% top-10 accuracy on 5,000 test reactions from USPTO patent data.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Liu 2017's sequence-to-sequence approach generates reactant SMILES freely, which can produce chemically invalid molecules; an alternative paradigm is to leverage known reaction templates and use neural networks to select the most appropriate template for a given target molecule.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Molecular fingerprint-based similarity model that retrieves and ranks reaction templates from a database of 40,000 precedent reactions; neural network learns to compute molecular similarity scores between target product and template preconditions; templates ensure chemical validity of predicted reactants.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 74.1% top-10 accuracy on 5,000 test reactions; the template-based approach guarantees chemically valid predictions and provides interpretable reaction types, complementing the template-free sequence approach of Liu 2017.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Constrained to templates present in the training database — cannot predict novel reaction types not seen during training; template extraction requires cheminformatics preprocessing; the fingerprint similarity metric is a shallow representation of molecular reactivity.

papers.title: Molecular Transformer: A Model for Uncertainty-Calibrated Chemical Reaction Prediction
papers.key_contribution: Molecular Transformer: replaces LSTM with self-attention Transformer for chemical reaction prediction; achieves 90.4% top-1 accuracy on forward reaction prediction and sets new SOTA for retrosynthesis; provides uncertainty calibration via beam search likelihood.

idea_genome.problem_genome: The LSTM encoder-decoder in Liu 2017 lacks the capacity to capture long-range dependencies in molecular SMILES; attention mechanisms from NLP (Transformer) could dramatically improve reaction prediction accuracy but have not been adapted to chemistry.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Standard Transformer encoder-decoder architecture applied to SMILES-to-SMILES translation; self-attention replaces LSTM recurrence, enabling direct modeling of long-range atom dependencies; beam search provides multiple predictions ranked by confidence; trained on USPTO reaction datasets.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 90.4% top-1 accuracy on forward reaction prediction (far surpassing LSTM); sets new SOTA for single-step retrosynthesis; beam search confidence correlates well with prediction accuracy, providing uncertainty estimates.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Treats molecules as flat SMILES strings without explicit 2D/3D molecular structure; retrosynthesis accuracy still limited by SMILES representation ambiguity; no interpretability of which atoms/bonds change; cannot explain reaction mechanisms.

papers.title: Retrosynthesis Prediction with Conditional Graph Logic Network
papers.key_contribution: Conditional Graph Logic Network (GLN): a conditional graphical model built on graph neural networks that treats reaction templates as logical rules and models the joint probability of rule selection and reactant generation; achieves state-of-the-art template-based retrosynthesis accuracy on USPTO-50k.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Coley 2017's fingerprint-based template retrieval uses shallow molecular representations; a deeper approach would treat template selection as probabilistic inference over a graphical model, jointly modeling the relationship between molecular graph structure, template applicability, and reactant feasibility.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Conditional graphical model combining graph neural networks with logical rules derived from reaction templates; models the joint probability P(template, reactants | product) using message passing on molecular graphs; templates are represented as first-order logic rules governing bond changes.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among template-based methods on USPTO-50k; the probabilistic graphical model provides principled uncertainty estimates and interpretable template selection; outperforms both fingerprint-based retrieval and pure sequence models.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Still constrained to pre-extracted reaction templates — cannot discover novel reaction types; template extraction and canonicalization require significant cheminformatics engineering; the graphical model inference is computationally expensive for large template libraries.

papers.title: Learning Graph Models for Retrosynthesis Prediction
papers.key_contribution: GraphRetro: a two-stage graph-based semi-template approach that first predicts graph edits (bond changes) to produce synthons, then completes synthons into full reactants by attaching leaving groups from a compact vocabulary; achieves 53.7% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k without reaction class.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Template-based methods (Coley 2017, GLN) are limited by pre-extracted templates; pure sequence models (Molecular Transformer) lack molecular graph awareness; a semi-template approach that decomposes retrosynthesis into graph edit prediction and synthon completion could combine the strengths of both paradigms.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Two-stage graph neural network: (1) Edit Prediction identifies bond changes on the product molecular graph to produce synthons (incomplete molecules), (2) Synthon Completion attaches leaving groups from a precomputed vocabulary (170 entries covering 99.7% of test set) to form complete reactants.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 53.7% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k (no reaction class), outperforming prior template-free and semi-template methods; the compact leaving group vocabulary demonstrates remarkable redundancy in organic chemistry reaction patterns.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: The leaving group vocabulary is fixed and dataset-dependent; the two-stage decomposition means errors in edit prediction cannot be corrected in the completion stage; accuracy still below template-based methods on certain reaction classes.

papers.title: Deep Retrosynthetic Reaction Prediction using Local Reactivity and Global Attention
papers.key_contribution: LocalRetro: template-based retrosynthesis guided by local atom/bond reactivity predictions; a graph neural network first identifies reactive atoms and bonds, then retrieves appropriate local reaction templates; achieves 89.5% top-1 round-trip accuracy and 53.4% top-1 exact match on USPTO-50k.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Prior template-based methods (GLN) apply templates globally to the entire molecule; chemical intuition suggests that reactions are local events centered on specific atoms and bonds, so predicting local reactivity first and then applying local templates should be more accurate and interpretable.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Graph neural network trained to predict atom-level and bond-level reactivity scores; local reaction templates are extracted and stored in atom-template and bond-template stores; at inference, the GNN identifies reactive sites and retrieves matching local templates to generate reactants.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 89.5% top-1 round-trip accuracy and 53.4% exact match on USPTO-50k; the local reactivity prediction aligns with chemical intuition about reaction mechanisms; at top-3 and top-5, LocalRetro outperforms all prior methods by significant margins.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Still relies on pre-extracted local templates — cannot predict reactions with novel local transformations; template extraction requires careful atom-mapping preprocessing; the local focus may miss long-range electronic effects that influence reactivity.

papers.title: Root-aligned SMILES: A Tight Representation for Chemical Reaction Prediction
papers.key_contribution: Root-aligned SMILES (R-SMILES): a molecular representation that aligns product and reactant SMILES strings by choosing a common root atom, reducing edit distance by 21% and enabling Transformer models to focus on chemical changes rather than syntactic differences; significantly boosts retrosynthesis prediction accuracy when combined with data augmentation.

idea_genome.problem_genome: The Molecular Transformer treats SMILES as arbitrary strings, but the same molecule can have many different SMILES representations depending on the starting atom; this ambiguity forces the model to learn complex syntax rules rather than focusing on chemical transformations.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Aligns product and reactant SMILES by selecting the atom with maximum common substructure as the root, creating a one-to-one mapping that minimizes edit distance; combined with 20x data augmentation at training and test time; applied to standard Transformer architecture.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Reduces minimum edit distance from 17.9 to 14.1 on USPTO-50k (21% reduction); significantly improves retrosynthesis prediction accuracy over standard SMILES; demonstrates that representation engineering can be as impactful as architectural innovation.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: The alignment requires atom-mapping between products and reactants, which is not always available; the data augmentation strategy is computationally expensive (20x); the representation improvement is specific to SMILES-based models and does not benefit graph-based approaches.

papers.title: Retroformer: Pushing the Limits of Interpretable End-to-end Retrosynthesis Transformer
papers.key_contribution: Retroformer: introduces a local attention head that jointly encodes molecular sequence and molecular graph, enabling the Transformer to focus on reactive regions while maintaining global context; achieves new SOTA for end-to-end template-free retrosynthesis.

idea_genome.problem_genome: The Molecular Transformer treats molecules as flat SMILES strings, missing the explicit 2D graph structure that determines reactivity; encoding both sequence and graph information could improve retrosynthesis prediction by directing attention to reactive bonds and atoms.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Adds a local attention head to the Transformer encoder that operates on the molecular graph adjacency; jointly encodes SMILES sequence (global context) and molecular graph (local reactive region); end-to-end template-free prediction without cheminformatics preprocessing.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves new state-of-the-art for end-to-end template-free retrosynthesis on USPTO-50k; improves molecule validity and reaction validity over Molecular Transformer; local attention provides interpretability by highlighting reactive bonds.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Graph encoding is limited to 2D molecular topology (no 3D conformation); still fundamentally a sequence-generation model with graph as auxiliary input; cannot handle multi-step retrosynthesis planning; accuracy gap with template-based methods on certain reaction classes.

papers.title: Retrosynthesis prediction using an end-to-end graph generative architecture for molecular graph editing
papers.key_contribution: Graph2Edits: end-to-end graph generative architecture that predicts retrosynthetic edits (bond breaking/forming) directly on the molecular graph, inspired by arrow-pushing formalism in reaction mechanisms; achieves 55.1% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k as a graph-native approach.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Retroformer and Molecular Transformer still fundamentally generate SMILES strings, which is an indirect representation of molecular changes; reaction mechanisms in chemistry are described as graph edits (arrow-pushing on bonds), suggesting a graph-native approach would be more natural and interpretable.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Graph neural network predicts sequential edits to the product molecular graph (bond breaking, bond forming, atom changes) in an autoregressive manner; combines graph-level perception with edit-level generation; inspired by the arrow-pushing formalism that chemists use to describe reaction mechanisms.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 55.1% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k for semi-template-based retrosynthesis (SOTA for graph-native methods); predictions are highly interpretable as they mirror the arrow-pushing notation chemists learn; handles complex reactions with multiple bond changes.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Graph-native generation is computationally expensive for complex molecules; 55.1% accuracy still below best template-based methods; limited to single-step retrosynthesis; the autoregressive edit generation can accumulate errors.

papers.title: RSGPT: a generative transformer model for retrosynthesis planning pre-trained on ten billion datapoints
papers.key_contribution: RSGPT: generative pretrained transformer for retrosynthesis, pretrained on 10 billion synthetically generated reaction datapoints; achieves 63.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k, substantially outperforming all previous methods; introduces reinforcement learning fine-tuning to capture product-reactant-template relationships.

idea_genome.problem_genome: Prior retrosynthesis models (Molecular Transformer, Retroformer, Graph2Edits) are trained on limited reaction datasets (50k-1M); LLM-scale pretraining on massive synthetic reaction data could dramatically improve accuracy, analogous to how GPT scaling transformed NLP.
idea_genome.mechanism_genome: Generative pretrained transformer pretrained on 10 billion computationally generated reaction datapoints; reinforcement learning fine-tuning aligns the model with chemical validity by rewarding correct product-reactant-template relationships; template-free SMILES-to-SMILES generation at inference.
idea_genome.observation_genome: Achieves 63.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k benchmark, substantially outperforming all prior methods; the 10B-scale pretraining combined with RL fine-tuning proves that LLM-scale data strategies can transform reaction prediction accuracy.
idea_genome.limitation_genome: Still limited to single-step retrosynthesis; 63.4% accuracy means 36.6% of predictions are incorrect, particularly for stereoselective and multi-step reactions; relies on computationally generated data whose distribution may differ from real experimental reactions; enormous pretraining compute cost.

edges.evolution_focus: Coley 2017 diverges from Liu 2017's template-free sequence generation to a fundamentally different paradigm — template-based retrieval using molecular fingerprint similarity. Both address single-step retrosynthesis but with incompatible mechanisms: one generates SMILES freely, the other ranks known reaction templates. This speciation event creates two parallel lineages.

Molecular Transformer replaces Liu 2017's LSTM encoder-decoder with a self-attention Transformer within the same template-free SMILES-to-SMILES retrosynthesis framework — a targeted architectural improvement that dramatically boosts accuracy (37% → 90%) while maintaining the core sequence translation formulation. Continues the template-free lineage.

GLN advances Coley 2017's template-based paradigm from fingerprint similarity retrieval to a conditional graphical model built on GNNs, replacing shallow fingerprint matching with deep probabilistic inference over template applicability — a significant mechanism improvement within the template-based lineage.

LocalRetro refines GLN's template-based GNN approach by shifting from global template selection to local atom/bond reactivity prediction, aligning template application with chemical intuition about local reaction sites — an incremental improvement that achieves superior top-k accuracy within the template-based lineage.

R-SMILES improves the Molecular Transformer's SMILES-based approach not through architectural changes but through a better molecular representation — root-aligned SMILES that reduce edit distance by 21%, demonstrating that representation engineering within the same Transformer framework can yield substantial accuracy gains. Continues the template-free lineage.

GraphRetro hybridizes ideas from both lineages: it uses graph neural network representations (from the GNN-based template methods like GLN) with a semi-template decomposition strategy that avoids full template dependence (from the template-free paradigm), combining graph-level edit prediction with a learned synthon completion step — bridging the template-based and template-free lineages.

Retroformer hybridizes the Molecular Transformer's SMILES sequence encoding with molecular graph structural encoding via a novel local attention head, jointly processing both representations to focus attention on chemically reactive regions while maintaining global sequence context — merging sequence-level and graph-level paradigms.

Graph2Edits refines GraphRetro's two-stage graph decomposition into a single end-to-end graph generative model that autoregressively predicts molecular edits, inspired by the arrow-pushing formalism — an incremental improvement within the graph-native semi-template paradigm, making generation fully end-to-end.

RSGPT hybridizes insights from both lineages: LLM-scale generative pretraining on 10 billion synthetic datapoints (scaling the template-free sequence approach to GPT-level data) with reinforcement learning fine-tuning that encodes template knowledge (product-reactant-template relationships from the template-based lineage), unifying both paradigms at unprecedented scale.

open_question: RSGPT achieves 63.4% top-1 accuracy through massive pretraining but still fails on over a third of reactions, particularly stereoselective transformations and multi-step routes. What mechanism would enable retrosynthesis models to jointly reason about single-step reactivity, stereochemical outcomes, reaction conditions, and multi-step synthetic planning in a unified end-to-end framework?

question.name: Question
question.description: Present only the domain/subfield and the curated May-2026 research question; ask the model to propose a next-step research direction in Computational Chemistry / Retrosynthesis Prediction.

library.name: Library
library.description: Present the curated research frontier plus 10 trace papers in random order with paper titles and brief abstracts, but without explicit lineage order, genome fields, or dynamics labels; ask the model to propose the next research direction.

lineage.name: Lineage
lineage.description: Present the curated research frontier plus the ordered trace with genome metadata, GenomeDiff-style change evidence, and parent-child relationships; ask the model to predict the next evolutionary step without naming predefined category labels.

frontier_evidence.title: RSGPT: a generative transformer model for retrosynthesis planning pre-trained on ten billion datapoints
frontier_evidence.unresolved_gap: Still limited to single-step retrosynthesis; 63.4% accuracy means 36.6% of predictions are incorrect, particularly for stereoselective and multi-step reactions; relies on computationally generated data whose distribution may differ from real experimental reactions; enormous pretraining compute cost.
frontier_evidence.why_frontier: Used as the May 2026 frontier anchor: the arena question targets a limitation that remains unresolved at the endpoint of this lineage.

frontier_status.manual_review: Re-audited on 2026-05-05 for appendix release: active arena trace has a 2025/2026 frontier endpoint, a non-solved open research question, valid forward lineage edges, and generation settings aligned with post-hoc dynamics evaluation.
frontier_status.prompt_role: The same research question is shown in Question, Library, and Lineage settings; only the supporting literature/lineage context changes.
